text: The video shows a scar on the right side of the abdomen, while the left side displays the deep inguinal ring, which is the target for dissection in the hernia repair procedure.

The surgeon uses a supraumbilical port for access, noting the patient's height affects the positioning, with the thumb positioned above the umbilicus. Two arcs are visible, measuring eighteen and twenty-four centimeters.
The video shows a scar from a previous open surgery on the right side of the patient's abdomen.
The surgeon positions the camera port at a 24-centimeter arc from the hernia site, aligning the thumb above the umbilicus, while referencing an 18-centimeter arc for orientation.

The surgical team positions the ports for the procedure, using a 36-centimeter instrument with ports placed 18 centimeters apart and 7.5 centimeters lateral on each side for the left and right hand. The telescope is visible, and the setup follows the baseball diamond concept.

The surgeon prepares to perform the procedure using an ipsilateral port position, with a manipulation angle of thirty degrees and azimuth angles of sixty and thirty degrees. A small stab wound is made on the camera port using an elaborate number knife.

The surgeon stands to the right of the patient and performs a varus needle entry using the left hand. The suction irrigation system is visible, and the hanging drop test is conducted, showing the drop formation.
The surgeon performs an irrigation test, confirming entry into the abdominal cavity. The tubing of the insufflator is attached, and pneumoperitoneum is initiated with a preset pressure of fifteen.
The insufflator is set to an initial flow rate of one liter per minute, which is then increased to three liters per minute, as the surgeon begins insufflating the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon removes the Veress needle after reaching a preset pressure of fifteen, ensuring a homogeneous distance within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon enlarges the initial 2mm stab wound at the camera port to a 10mm incision, preparing for further surgical access.

The surgeon introduces the trocar into the abdominal cavity using a screwing motion, holding it like a pistol with the middle finger wrapped around the air inlet. After insertion, the surgeon checks for gas leakage through the trocar and attaches the insufflator, setting the flow rate to a maximum of ten liters per minute.

The optical trocar is inserted, followed by the introduction of the telescope to visually inspect the entry point for any inadvertent injury. The surgeon then identifies the hernia site. A secondary port is placed 7.5 centimeters lateral from an arc measuring 18 centimeters, designated as the left-hand port.
The surgeon places another secondary port, positioned 7.5 centimeters lateral from the previous port, using a trocar.
The surgeon inserts the telescope through the optical trocar, providing a clear view of the surgical field. The inferior epigastric vessel is visible, requiring careful navigation to avoid injury.
The surgeon positions a 7.5 cm port ipsilaterally, with all ports now in place. The right hand holds a harmonic scalpel, ready to begin the hernia repair procedure.

The surgeon begins the hernia repair procedure using a grasper in the left hand, while the right hand operates a harmonic scalpel. The surgical instruments are positioned through the lateral ports under visual guidance.
The operating room light is set to twenty lux, illuminating the hernia site clearly.
The surgeon visually identifies the left-sided indirect inguinal hernia, with the anatomical structures clearly visible under the surgical lights.
The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessel, which is visible in the surgical field during the hernia repair procedure.
The medial umbilical ligament is visible in the surgical field, providing a reference point for anatomical orientation during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon visually identifies the triangle of doom and triangle of pain, anatomical regions associated with the indirect inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon clearly defines the anatomical area, highlighting the median umbilical ligament positioned on the right side of the surgical field.
The medial umbilical ligament is visible in the surgical field, identifiable as a distinct anatomical structure.

The surgeon begins dissection above the arc of the hernia, starting from the medial umbilical ligament and proceeding six centimeters laterally to the outer margin of the defect at the ten o'clock position.

The surgeon begins dissection for left hernia repair from the medial umbilical ligament, located just above the arc of the hernia defect, and extends laterally up to six centimeters from the outer margin of the defect.

At the ten o'clock position, the surgeon carefully dissects near the inferior epigastric vessel using a harmonic scalpel, pushing the vessel towards the anterior abdominal wall while progressing six centimeters laterally.

The surgeon prepares to place a mesh, selecting a size of fifteen by fifteen or fifteen by twelve centimeters for a three-centimeter defect. A pre-peritoneal incision is made, and dissection begins with the aim of addressing the triangle of pain, avoiding the hernia sac initially.
The surgeon performs lateral peritoneal dissection in the triangle of pain, gradually breaking away areolar tissue to create a pocket suitable for placing a large mesh.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection within the triangle of pain, carefully separating areolar tissue to create a pocket for mesh placement.
The surgeon continues the hernia repair using blunt dissection, minimizing the use of energy sources to carefully separate tissue layers.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the triangle of pain, having separated the areolar tissue and prepared the area for mesh placement.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the triangle of pain, visibly separating the areolar tissue to create a pocket for mesh placement.
The surgeon identifies the spermatic vessel as the medial boundary of the triangle of pain during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon reaches the spermatic vessel, indicating the completion of the dissection of the triangle of pain and lateral dissection.

The surgeon proceeds with the medial dissection, targeting the trapezoid of disaster area. This involves dissecting medial to the inferior epigastric vessel and pushing the medial umbilical ligament medially.

The surgeon uses both hands to separate the bladder downward, revealing the Cooper's ligament, which appears as a pearly white structure reflecting light. The Cooper's ligament is visible and serves as a landmark in the surgical field.
The surgeon pushes the Cooper ligament and bladder inferiorly and medially, revealing the space of Retzius.
The surgical field displays the Cupid ligament, inferior epigastric vessels, urinary bladder, and sigmoid colon, with these anatomical structures clearly visible.
The space of Radius is clearly visible, showing a distinct anatomical area during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon defines the retropubic space, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon displays the space of Retzius, highlighting the shiny Cooper's ligament visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the medial dissection, with the retropubic space and space of radius previously defined and visible.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection followed by medial dissection, and proceeds to dissect the hernia sac, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon uses the left hand to pull the peritoneal sac while the right hand pushes the pseudo sac, which is the transversalis fascia. In the case of an indirect hernia, the peritoneum is carefully separated from the vas deferens and spermatic vessels, with the sac being meticulously extracted.
The surgeon visually identifies the indirect hernia, highlighting its anatomical location and characteristics within the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the hernia defect located lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel and above the inguinal ligament, highlighting the anatomical position of the defect in relation to these structures.
The surgeon carefully separates the hernia sac, pulling it with one hand while maintaining tension to facilitate dissection.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens medially and the spermatic vessel laterally during the dissection of the hernia sac.
The surgeon carefully performs dissection, pushing the transversalis fascia back into the defect while separating the peritoneum from the vas deferens and spermatic vessels.

The surgeon pushes the transversalis fascia, identified as the pseudo sac, back into its original position within the defect, located lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel and above the inguinal ligament.
The surgeon identifies a fat pad at the level of the neck of the hernia sac and carefully pushes adjacent structures away.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers the hernia sac, ensuring not to disturb the surrounding structures: the inferior epigastric vessel above, the vas deferens medially, and the spermatic vessel laterally. The pseudo sac, formed by the transversalis fascia, is visible above. Occasionally, the lower flap is lifted to enter the defect from outside, revealing the extent of the sac.
The surgeon holds the hernia sac from the outside, visually assessing its boundaries and the extent of dissection performed.
The surgeon uses the right hand to push back the transversal fascia while the left hand holds the hernia sac, manipulating these structures to expose the surgical field.
The surgeon performs peritilization, with the peritoneum being carefully separated from the vas deferens and the spermatic vessel.
The surgical field shows the hernia sac fully exposed, with the transversus fascia pushed back, creating space for mesh placement.
The surgeon removes the hernia sac, resulting in the disappearance of the previously visible defect, and revealing additional peritoneum.

The surgeon demonstrates the separation of the hernia sac from the transversalis fascia, with the sac visible below. For a left-sided hernia, the monitor is positioned near the patient's right shoulder, and the surgeon stands near the right hip.
The surgeon clears the vas deferens and spermatic vessels, visible medially in the surgical field, while standing near the patient's right hip with the monitor positioned near the left shoulder.

The surgeon carefully separates the peritoneum from the spermatic vessel and vas deferens, ensuring no buttonhole is created. The peritoneum is progressively separated, maintaining a six-centimeter margin from the defect's lower edge.
The surgeon identifies the triangle of pain area, with visible nerves clearly discernible within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the triangle of doom area, where the iliac vessels are visible and separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon exposes the peritoneum below the surgical field, continuing the separation process from the surrounding structures.

The surgeon uses two atraumatic graspers to perform blunt dissection, pushing the spermatic vessel away from the peritoneum to enlarge the pocket. One grasper holds the peritoneum while the other separates the vas deferens and spermatic vessel from the transversalis fascia.
The surgeon enlarges the triangle of doom, a region containing the iliac vessels, by carefully separating surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the separation of the peritoneum from the triangle of doom, resulting in a wider opening of the area, indicating the completion of this dissection step.
The surgeon separates the vas deferens medially from surrounding tissues within the triangle of doom area, using atraumatic graspers to carefully manipulate and isolate the structure.
The surgeon identifies the anatomical landmarks: the triangle of doom, the triangle of pain, the epigastric vessel, and the pubic ligaments within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens and spermatic vessels, noting the expanded space of Retzius in the surgical field.
The surgeon points out the trapezoid of disaster, an anatomical area that requires careful observation during the procedure.
The iliac vessel is visible between anatomical landmarks, including the triangle of doom and triangle of pain, with surrounding structures clearly defined.
The surgeon highlights the visible pulsation of the iliac vessel, which can be observed in the surgical field.
The surgeon visually identifies the spermatic vessel within the surgical field.
The surgeon gently pulls the spermatic vessel away, revealing the iliac vessel beneath it.
The surgeon points out the iliac vessel, highlighting its location and visibility within the surgical field.
The external iliac artery and vein are visible in the surgical field, positioned adjacent to the previously identified iliac vessel.
The surgeon identifies the spatial orientation of the iliac vessels, with the vein positioned medially and the artery laterally.
The surgeon opens the space between the external iliac artery and vein, revealing the anatomical separation between these two vessels.
A well-defined pocket is visible between the external iliac artery and vein, with the vein positioned medially and the artery laterally.
The surgeon exposes the external iliac artery, with its pulsation visibly observed, positioned laterally to the external iliac vein.
The completed dissection reveals the anatomical space between the external iliac artery and vein, with clear visualization of these structures.
The external iliac vessel is visible in the surgical field, showing its anatomical position and pulsation.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens, a tubular structure, within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the spermatic vessel within the surgical field, highlighting its anatomical position relative to previously dissected structures.

The surgical field reveals the formation of anatomical spaces including the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster. The inferior epigastric vessel is visible, and the hernia sac has been completely removed.
The surgeon forms a pocket around the outer margin of the hernia defect, measuring six centimeters in diameter.
The surgeon identifies and pulls down a small amount of tissue over the vas deferens, which is visually distinct in the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the space of Retzius, which is a potential space located behind the pubic bone and in front of the bladder, often accessed during hernia repair procedures.
The surgeon identifies the coronamortis vein in the surgical field, noting the presence of an aberrant obturator vessel in the area.
The surgeon exposes the bladder area, revealing its anatomical position within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the tenderness arc of the levator ani muscle, which can occasionally be observed during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the white line located below the Cooper's ligament, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessels, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the spermatic vessel, which is visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens, a tubular structure visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes that the pocket created during the hernia repair procedure is firm, indicating successful tissue manipulation and reinforcement.
The surgeon prepares to place the mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring proper coverage and fixation within the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to place a transabdominal preperitoneal mesh, selecting a mesh size that accommodates the twelve plus defect visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon visually identifies the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve (LFCN) and the genitofemoral nerve (GFN) within the surgical field.
The lateral femoral cutaneous nerve of the thigh and the genital branch of the genitofemoral nerve are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon completes the dissection, revealing the arc of the transversus abdominis muscle and a short muscle above it. The mesh is then pushed laterally into the pocket, ensuring it covers the area completely. The mesh is characterized by its adequate size, memory, and ultra-lightweight properties.
The surgeon positions a large mesh, sized twelve plus defect, ensuring it unfolds and spreads evenly with its center aligned to the center of the hernia defect.

The surgeon positions the center of the mesh over the center of the defect to minimize recurrence risk. Using a titanium tacker, the first fixation is applied to the Cooper's ligament, visible as a white structure at the inferior medial end.

The surgeon fixes the mesh laterally onto the transversus abdominis muscle. Following this, the peritoneum is closed over the mesh, and the lower margins are secured. The gas pressure in the surgical field is reduced to between eight and twelve millimeters of mercury.
The surgeon uses a surgical instrument to manipulate the peritoneum, covering the entire defect area for peritonization.
The surgeon covers the mesh, ensuring it is properly positioned over the hernia defect.
The surgeon demonstrates the option to use suturing for securing the mesh in place during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the use of a Dandy jamming knot for continuous suturing, focusing on the termination of the suture line.
The surgeon uses a tacker to secure the mesh in place, covering the hernia defect with visible precision.
The surgeon visually identifies the sigmoid colon during the procedure.
The surgeon decreases the pressure to eight millimeters of mercury, causing the anterior abdominal wall to visibly move inward.
The surgeon uses the left hand to oppose the anterior abdominal wall, ensuring the tacker is fired perpendicular to the wall for secure mesh placement.
The surgical field shows the entire hernia defect covered by the mesh, indicating successful placement over the defect area.
As the gas is released, the peritoneum visibly settles and touches any remaining space within the surgical field.
The surgical field shows no visible mesh or defect, indicating the completion of the hernia repair.
